title: Indefinite integrals: eˣ & 1/x
description: For example, find the indefinite integral of 4eˣ-3/x.
topic_path: Finding antiderivatives and integrals: basic rules and notation: common indefinite integrals

hints:
We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(5e^x-\dfrac2x \right)dx
\\\\
&=5\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx-2\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=5e^x-2\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac3x+2e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=3\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx+2\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=3\ln|x|+2e^x+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(-3e^x+\dfrac4x \right)dx
\\\\
&=-3\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx+4\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=-3e^x+4\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac2x-4e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=2\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx-4\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=2\ln|x|-4e^x+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(4e^x-\dfrac5x \right)dx
\\\\
&=4\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx-5\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=4e^x-5\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(-\dfrac3x+3e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=-3\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx+3\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=-3\ln|x|+3e^x+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(6e^x+\dfrac7x \right)dx
\\\\
&=6\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx+7\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=6e^x+7\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac5x+e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=5\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx+\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=5\ln|x|+e^x+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(-2e^x-\dfrac6x \right)dx
\\\\
&=-2\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx-6\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=-2e^x-6\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(-\dfrac1x+7e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=-1\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx+7\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=-\ln|x|+7e^x+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(-5e^x+\dfrac4x \right)dx
\\\\
&=-5\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx+4\int\dfrac1x \,dx
\\\\
&=-5e^x+4\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

We can integrate using the following formulas for the indefinite integrals of $e^x$ and $\dfrac1x$:

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C
\\\\
&\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx=\ln|x|+C
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac5x-3e^x \right)dx
\\\\
&=5\displaystyle\int \dfrac1x\,dx-3\int e^x \,dx
\\\\
&=5\ln|x|-3e^x+C
\end{align}$
